And now, the conclusion of our conversation with Brad Peterson as he shares the most powerful lessons he's discovered to help you avoid many of the common blunders made by marketers today. Let's listen in on part two. I think, you know, it's hard for me to tell, but I think I was on just a rant about getting your foundational principles correct, you know, before you start going off on trying to entertain people, trying to be funny, trying to be clever, and so on. You know, you can do all those things after you've got the basics down and test against, you know, more traditional principles. If you're convinced that your entertainment-oriented ad is going to kick butt, then you do that only after you've got something proven. Yeah. Okay, well, we can start here. I was wanting to say something. I want to really draw you out and let them hear your voice, but there's something that keeps coming back to me, and it's in conversations I've had with Wendy. And that is, you know, an ad needs to, in order to be successful, in order to make a sale, any kind of ad, including any TV ad, has to do at the very minimum two things. It has to create a desire for the product. And then secondly, it has to give the prospect a way to acquire that product. And the ads that we're seeing so much of right now on television, the kinds of image advertising things where the images don't connect with the product in any way. You gave an example of an ad you had seen, I think it was for a URL registration service. Right, yeah, godaddy.com. Godaddy.com, where it doesn't talk about what you can do with your URL, the benefits of why you would, you know, why you would want an internet site. It doesn't create desire for the product. And because it's vampire video, because you remember everything but the name of the company, it doesn't even give you a way to acquire the product. And so it's a failure at the two most basic levels of advertising. Right. And I suspect most of the people that actually then logged on to the site or who remembered the site remembered it so they could go look at the girl as opposed to going there to actually do some business. Because the site has, you know, additional video of her and pictures of her and links to her website and all this stuff. And it's all about her, this beautiful woman. Yeah. So, you know, if highly paid advertising agencies can't do the two most basic things that advertising is supposed to do, then, you know, you don't have to run the ads to know that they're going to be a total failure. You don't create desire and if you don't help them acquire, then we should come up with a good rhyme there. If it doesn't fit, you must acquit. Right. If there's no desire and if you don't help them acquire, then... Yeah, you don't have a buyer. Then the agency should be fired. But those are the two most basic things, you know. Accountability and tracking are important too. But you don't need them to look at an ad that creates no desire for the product and no way for the... And where the video or the rest of the ad kind of totally occupies the mind so that you don't remember the name of the product. You don't need tracking to know that that's a dog from the word go. Right. Right. Okay, so you want me to extrapolate on that a little bit? Is that what you're looking for? Go for it. Okay. I would say, I mean, it's a major problem in all advertising, not just TV advertising, is the lack of... You know, they're not creating a desire for the product often because they're not even telling you what the product is or they're simply naming the product and not telling you why you should own it. And they're also not making any kind of distinct offer. They're not telling you, you can buy this for this much, here's how you get it, here's your guarantee, here's your free bonuses, and so on and so forth. I mean, the model, especially when it comes down to something like TV advertising where I think this is most prevalent. If you go to one of those long format infomercials, 20, 30 minute infocommercials, these guys are selling you and selling you and selling you, and then they're giving you a specific offer, send us $29.95 a month for three months and we're going to give you this incredible product that does all these things for you, and you're going to get this bonus and this bonus and this bonus. And call this specific phone number or log on to this website. Selling is left to chance and too many advertisers on TV and in print just leave too much to chance. They throw out some cleverness or some funny idea or some entertaining thing, but they don't really make it clear what they're selling, why you should own it, why you must have it. I mean, it's not just why you should own it, I mean, you need to make your product indispensable. It's not enough to just have someone say, oh yeah, I guess I'd kind of like to have that sometime in the future. You got to make them want it right now, I got to have it. It's not something I can pass up, it's an offer that's going to disappear and I may miss it forever. Those are the kind of thoughts and feelings you want going on in your customers. And if you have a product that connects, you know, that they really want, that's going to happen as long as you sell it. If you don't sell it, you have no hope of connecting with someone even if they're desperate for your product. You know, I could be desperate, for instance, let's go back to that godaddy.com thing. You know, say I'm paying 30, 40, 50 bucks a month or a year to register my domain site, my internet domain site, or I've got, say, 10 or 20 of them. So I spend three, four, $500 a year on my domain site and I know I've gone on to godaddy and I know you can get them for like 10 bucks or 7 bucks or some, you know, much cheaper price. But if you don't tell me that, I can save, you know, two thirds of what I'm spending now, I never know and I'm not going to go there just to look at some bucks a woman if I'm a serious businessman. Now, if I'm a teenage boy who has no need for a domain site, yeah, I'm going to jump on that site and take a look at her, but then I'm not going to buy the product. So the key is to make sure your customer, you know, your prospects, you know, they know what you're selling and inflame in them a desire for what you're selling by appealing to their emotions, by showing them the benefits you offer and by making them a specific offer, how they can get it, how fast and how much it is. And that really, I mean, it really comes down to that. If you can get that right, you know, you're on your way and then you can go forth and test the stuff that I call the nonsense, you know, you want to be funny and you want to be clever and entertaining and do that kind of thing, fine. Test that against your proven ad and see what happens. And 99% of the time you're going to find that no matter how clever your idea is, it's going to lose to something that's proven that's, you know, based on the traditional old style direct marketing principles. Well, I think you're right. And, you know, we're really thanking Godaddy.com here. There are many others like them, but I think this interview basically is going to turn out to be kind of a study in how to do it wrong. Right. And I wonder if you could get a copy of that ad. We could offer it as an MPG. Well, it's on their website. For download. I mean, it's on their website. You know, if you can get permission to let people download it, you can certainly link to that site, I would think. Well, okay. I think the copyright laws will allow us to distribute that ad. Could be. Could be. But it's on their site. And they've also got a longer internet version that's even more, even raceier. And they've got more stuff coming out for this year's Super Bowl. But yeah, you know, we're, you're right. We're picking on Godaddy.com, but that's just because that's what came to mind. It's almost like a case study. And it's the most famous. It's the one everyone, if I talk to someone about the Super Bowl, that's really the ad they remember. Well, the, so you bring up another point. And that is that most advertising agencies will at least attempt to identify your prospect market. Okay. Hang on. Will, will at least identify your prospect market. And then craft the message in a way that speaks to those people. Right. And that's one thing that most ad agencies do quite well. And it seems like with this Godaddy.com thing, first of all, even the name of the site tells me this is not a serious business site. Right. Okay. Secondly, the name of the site doesn't tell me what they do. Godaddy doesn't say anything about their product, why it's superior, you know, it gives me no information at all. And then thirdly, all of that tends to be, you know, shows me that the people behind the product are not serious, are not, don't understand the importance of crafting a product for a particular niche. So they're selling cheap websites to people who, as you said, you know, using advertising that's targeted at folks that can't afford them. Right. Right. Well, folks who just don't need domain sites. They don't need them. I'm looking for the video now. Go ahead. Okay. Yeah. Any teenager can afford one of their sites, but how many teenagers need it? You know, and they're not making money at 7.99 a site. You know, they've got all kinds of add-on services. You know, I think their business is mostly the back-end stuff, but if you're just attracting people who aren't even interested in your front-end stuff, you're never going to sell them your back-end stuff. And by back-end, I mean products you sell to existing customers after they've made their initial sale, which we'll also want to get into later because that's another big, big opportunity that a lot of businesses miss out on. And I'm not sure what else to say about this on this topic, Clayton. Okay, go ahead then. Let's move on. The thing to move on to probably would be, I think we're down to, unless you had something you wanted to stick in here, was the next question on my list was what two or three lessons from all this can help a business person improve his or her advertising? And we can go into that if you like. Okay, so do you want me to just go with that? Yeah, we'll write the question later. Okay, yeah. I mean, the key thing, you know, and I've said it probably a dozen times in this interview, is to recognize that all your advertising, all your marketing is selling. You're not trying to do anything else but make a sale. And you would no more have a salesman walk in front of one of your customers and dance a jig and tell jokes and, you know, whatever, take off their top, do a strip tease, all that stuff. No salesman would ever do that. So generally, you're not, you don't want to do that in your advertising because it's just not effective. You want your advertising to reflect what your top salesperson would say if they were standing in front of a prospect. You know, what exactly would you do to sell a guy? And it's not going to be to entertain them. It's going to be to say, hey, here's my product and here's what you get when you buy it and you get all this great stuff and you've got to have it. So, you know, it involves finding reasons for your prospects to do business with you. I mean, giving them a reason. I think I might have mentioned earlier that Claude Hopkins, when he observed the advertising around him, most business people were saying nothing more than, give me the business you've been giving to other people. You know, buy for me instead of them and that's the extent of it. And that's no reason at all. That doesn't give anyone a reason to change the way they're doing things. So you've got to give them reasons why, just as the salesman would. So that would be, that would be, I guess, my first lesson. The second lesson would be to actually prove that you're going to deliver what you're offering. You know, a lot of businessmen are content just to make an offer, list all these benefits, but all your prospects, especially these days, they just don't believe it. They're saying, yeah, right, okay, you know, I've seen this before, I've been screwed before, it's not going to happen. So you need to prove to them that what you're offering is really legit and really true. And you do that with testimonials. The more specific, the better. You do that with case studies. Clients who have actually benefited from your product and you show how they benefited. You do it by offering a terrific guarantee. I find that a lot of people, for some of you, a lot of business people are really averse to guarantees. They're just afraid people are going to take advantage of them. And I'll tell you right up front, people are going to take advantage of business people who offer guarantees. But those few scumbags that are going to take advantage of you are going to be more than overrun by the increased response you get to your offer. I know Clayton, you've got a great example of this from when you were working with Martin Weiss and you convinced them to, I think, switch from a 90-day to a one-year guarantee. And yeah, your refund rate went up, but your sales went up by some, you know, you'll have to tell me how much it was, but by some astronomical amount that made it well worth your while. Well, actually what happened, Brad, was they had a 60-day guarantee. This is a perfect example of how Penny Weiss can make you pound foolish. They had a 60-day money-back guarantee. So you sign up for this $100 a year newsletter and you have a very short, a very small window of time to determine whether or not you're going to ask for a refund, to determine whether or not you're satisfied. And people remember things over the short term. And so as soon as they bought, every single person who bought our product had this mental date. 60 days from that day, they could still get their money back and keep all of the free gifts and keep all the issues they'd received in the meantime. And so they had a fairly high cancellation rate at exactly 59 days after every major promotion. So my argument to them was that we could get a higher response rate by making it a one-year guarantee. And of course I would have liked to have done unlimited. But my argument to them was that if you can ask for a refund at any time during your subscription, that we'd get a higher response rate and that we would get fewer cancellations at that 59-day mark. And it took forever to get them to even test it. But when they did, they didn't believe the results. And so we tested it a second time. And then after the second series of tests, finally they realized that setting a short time limit on the guarantee actually increases refund requests and lowers response. Right. That's a great example. And most businessmen I think will find similar things happening to them in their business. And I'll give you, one of the things I left out of my story about my pool queue client, Q&K sales was, when we first started selling their queues, initially I could see that there actually didn't take much convincing. He was remarkably open-minded to this. I said, let's guarantee that the dealers will actually sell these queues if they take them. Now they pay for them up front, put them in their store. And if no one buys them, they get to return them and we'll give them all their money back and even pay for the shipping, which is really quite a chunk. Whenever you buy a dozen or 25 pool queues, the shipping isn't cheap. And so a lot of dealers, yeah, maybe they can get their money back, but they've got to pay that shipping charge. So we paid, we ate that as well. And we kind of think we called it something like our unlimited sales guarantee that they, you know, if anytime within the first year, if they didn't sell the queue or they hated the queue or whatever, you know, they could just return it back, no, return it back to us, no questions asked. Now we never actually tested anything without the guarantee. So I don't know what that did to our sales, but I do know that it wasn't long before virtually every major competitor was offering exactly the same thing. And up until that time, to my knowledge, no one had ever offered a guarantee like that it just wasn't done. You know, the pool, the dealers had to take their chances. They took the queues and prayed that they would sell them. And with our offer, I mean, clearly, if you're trying to decide, do I want to carry, you know, low priced QA or low priced QB and A comes with a guarantee and B doesn't, your choice is a no brainer. You're going to take the one with the guarantee. And yeah, I mean, if your product is crappy, if your service is crappy, if what you're selling is junk, then guarantee is going to stick it to you. But my client had a good product. It delivered on its promises and he delivered on his promises. And so we weren't getting boxes and boxes and queues back. I mean, they took the queues, they sold them and our advertising helped them sell them. And it turned out, you know, it's been, I don't know, 10, 15 years since we started and he still has the almost exact same guarantees modified it slightly for some products. But he pretty much guarantees everything he sells and now he sells everything under the sun, queues, cases, bar stools, cab, all kinds of anything related to like home entertainment and, but I shouldn't, I shouldn't call it home entertainment. Any, anything related to having a pool room in your house, all the cabinetry, all the stairs, all the bars for the chairs, all the bars, all that stuff. And he guarantees all of it. And it's just worked like gangbusters. Did you test premiums with that client? Ah, did we test premiums? I don't recall if we've ever tested premiums. We haven't really done a lot of direct offerings to the end user. We've only done it to the, to the, mostly to the dealers. And I realize that doesn't, you know, we can use premiums. No, we really haven't. I don't think we have. Okay. Okay, cool. But that's a good idea. I should suggest that. But again, like I said, they're now under the sway of the evil graphic designer and pretty much don't listen to anything I say anymore. Okay. Oh, and the next lesson then after, after at least trying a guarantee. Um, is to, you know, like we've taught when we've hit this over the head of getting into is tracking, track everything you do, track your results, you know, do anything you can to figure out if your ads working. Now, obviously not every ad is a direct response. Adam, if you're a retailer, you can use coupons. Uh, you know, at the very least have your customers or your customers have your employees ask your customers, how'd you hear about us? Where'd you get our name? You know, that's the least you do. At least it's better than nothing. Cause if you, you know, if you're running, if you're spending, you know, thousands of dollars to run a daily newspaper ad, how do you know you're getting anything out of it? No one is asking the customers where they came from. You don't have a coupon. You don't have a response device. You know, you're not having them come in and request something specific in the ad like a free report or, you know, some kind of free premium item, just something, you know, come in and get a free six pack of soda for crying out loud, whatever it is. You know, figure out a way to track your results. So you know what's working and what's not working. And then the final thing is, is, you know, asking for the order. So many people just, they get to the end of the pitch and it's the old, you know, salesman thing of closing the sale. They get to the end of the pitch and they just stop and go away. And they never bothered to say, okay, how many would you like? When do you want them delivered? And so on and so forth. And you need to do the same thing in your advertising. Tell them, you know, here's the deal. It's, you know, it's, it's X dollars. You know, like I said a minute ago, it's X dollars. It's here's your guarantee. Here's how to get it. And tell them what to do to get it. You know, come over to, you know, whatever, Joe's TV store and, and take a look. We're located at, given the address. Don't assume they're going to look up the address or look up the phone number. Don't bury it at the bottom of the ad. I see ads like that all the time. It's a great sales pitch, but then the address and phone number are like in a letterhead at the bottom of the ad, which is crazy. People don't want to have to work their way through your advertising. They want to, you know, have it come to a logical conclusion. They see your phone number or they see your address. And they know exactly what to do to take advantage of your offer. I think most people are unaware. Just, you know, they tend to think people are looking at their advertising. They want to read their advertising. They don't want to read your advertising. They're not interested in your advertising. Once you've got their attention, you know, they're, they're tired. They're worn out after a hard day's work or, you know, they're distracted by their wife and kids. So if they're looking at your ad, you don't want to make them work any harder than they absolutely have to to get the information they need. So you got to make everything crystal clear. And if you do those things, you know, and even if you don't do them brilliantly, you are going to be so far ahead of your competitors. It's not even going to be funny. Uh, you know, you can do this stuff. If you do it right, you can do this stuff mediocre in a lot of markets where there's no one doing anything right. And you, you, you'll be the king. And then you'll have the opportunity to start really, you know, perfecting everything and bringing in people like Clayton, make peace and paying for a quick crit that will drive your advertising, you know, uh, the value of your advertising up. And, you know, soon, you know, you find yourself dominating your market. And I, and it's, it just breaks my heart to see people going out of business who could be the king of their market. If they would just follow principles, you know, that have been around since 19, you know, 1902 and are just simply ignored in favor of, um, you know, entertaining people, so to speak. I'll give you one, one neat technique for doing this. Cause a lot of people, you know, not everyone can sit down and write an ad. And not everyone has the means to hire a high priced copywriter. So it's, it's something that Gary Halbert advocated that I think is, is really a great strategy. And what he said to do was to get your best salesman and tape record his presentations or his sales pitch or however you do it on the phone in person and tape record him again and again and again until he's no longer cognizant of the tape recorder. He's not, you know, he's, he's gotten used to the idea that he's being taped and keep working on it. So, you know, you've got a tape of him at what Gary called concert pitch where he's just his most brilliant. And then you simply transcribe that sales pitch. You clean it up, you know, get rid of the us and Oz and homes. And it can almost practically become your sales letter. And that's one down and dirty, simple, easy, cheap way to get a good sales pitch in the mail. And it's something that, you know, I don't, I don't know if anyone's ever followed that advice, but it's sure something that, uh, I think has got a lot of value, especially if you don't have, um, you know, if you don't have the means to hire a copywriter. I think that's great advice. And, you know, just actually one of the themes that, that keeps coming back and back in this interview is this, the idea that advertising is salesmanship. And, um, uh, even if you don't have a salesman, even if you're the person who does the sales yourself, the point of the matter is that your letter is a salesman for you. And, uh, you, the letter needs to accomplish everything that a sale, a live salesman would accomplish in approaching the subject, the prospect, getting his attention, um, presenting the benefits of the product, justifying the price, asking for the sale and, and, uh, even taking the check. And so, um, yeah, absolutely. It applies brilliantly. Um, we're, uh, running a little long. I want to, um, ask you the, the, the next question on my list is what two or three lessons from all of this could help a business person improve his advertising? Let's sum up. Okay. Well, I want to, let me, before, before I completely answer that, let me throw in a couple of things that I want to add that I think are really important beyond because I think it's something that businessmen can easily do that they're missing out on. And that is every businessman I talk to is, I shouldn't say every, I always have a tendency to say every most of the business people I talk to are completely focused on getting new customers. I need more customers. I need more customers. And they're simply ignoring the customers they already have. They're putting them in on back burner. They're no longer important because they already bought something and they're taken for granted. So I would urge every businessman man listening to this tape, to this interview to come up with new offers for your, for your current customers, new ways to sell them. That's where the real money is. You spend a lot of money bringing a customer into your business. Once you've got them, don't let them get away. You know, keep selling them stuff. The second thing is when you're getting new customers, most businessmen are focused for some reason on, I've got to make a profit on every new customer I get. But if you can calculate that every customer you get is worth some amount of money to you in the future, it's worth it to lose money to acquire a customer. And you see that stores do it all the time when they offer Dr. Pepper, you know what it is. $1.99 a six pack or $1.99 12 pack and they're losing money, but they're getting you in the store where you buy $200 worth of groceries where they're making their profit. And the principle is the same in any business where you get them in the store with a low ball offer, you get them on your customer list with a low ball offer and then you sell the heck out of them. And there is, you know, and you can, you can work through what each customer's worth to you over the long run on average. And then you can easily calculate what you can spend to bring them in the door. Then the final thing is nobody contacts their customers often enough. Everyone's afraid. Oh, if I keep in touch with my customers, they'll get mad at me. And it's just not true. Uh, you can, you can contact your customers, you know, weekly, monthly, you know, whatever it is more often than you do now. I'll almost guarantee you. If you doubled those contacts, your sales are going to increase dramatically. And I'll give you an example. I had a client, a nutrition client who's had nutrition stores and also sold through the mail and he had a mailer that was like a sale mailer where everything went on sale like twice a year. And he called up Gary Halbert and said, Gary, what can I do to increase sales of this flyer? He was paying Gary some ungodly amount of money. And Gary said, just mail it again 30 days later. Now the client was furious. This was the big advice he was paying for, but his sales more than doubled. So he suddenly was getting, you know, X and now he's getting two X and all he done was mail the thing again 30 days later. And you know, there's a valuable lesson there for any, anybody just to keep, you know, contacting their customers. I know Clayton, you once said that when you did your blanchard promotions, everyone told you, you're going to burn out the list. You can't sell to them so often. You're going to, you know, turn them off the blanchard. And the next thing you know, you were mailing every week and the, and the, and the response was, was even more impressive than when you were mailing every month. Yeah. So almost any businessman is going to benefit by contacting their customers more often. So let me go in, um, to that question you asked, what kind of client do to help me get, get, uh, that would help me to help them more. And really it comes down to the stuff we've talked about. Give me reasons why someone should buy your product. You know, tell, you know, come up with that stuff. You've got to really, you've really got to put your mind to it. Why would someone want this product and how can, how can my product connect with the emotions that they're experiencing right now? You know, what, why do they want the product? What emotional need is it answering or what emotions are they feeling that I can, uh, you know, help, help them with and, you know, make your product connect with the, tell me how your product connects with those things. And, you know, some of the obvious stuff, you know, come up with a unique selling proposition or work with me to come up with a unique selling proposition. If you're the same as everybody else, it's going to be awfully hard to distinguish yourself on the marketplace. So in a copywriter, can't just make this stuff up. You know, we've, we've got to have the client's help to, to come up with ways to make the product new, different, unique, you know, to connect with the emotions, to get all the benefits out there. The next thing would be to, to work on the amount of proof you give. I mean, I don't know how many clients I've worked with where I said, okay, have you got any testimonials? Nope, not one case studies, zero. Uh, you know, do you offer a guarantee yet? 10 days, you know, and I can't go out there and make an offer and say, oh, this product's going to make you rich, but I don't have one testimonial. I don't have one case study and I'm not willing to guarantee it. It, it, it, that those are the component, those are the, that's what's needed to have a failing promotion. So spend a considerable amount of time gathering testimonials from your clients and clients and customers and make them give you testimonials that are as specific as possible, that they exactly results they got, not just, Hey, Joe, you're great. Thanks a lot. That's, that's virtually worthless. You need, you know, Hey, Joe, your vacuum cleaner made my carpet 84% cleaner than it ever was before. And obviously that's a silly example, but it's got the specifics that are needed for it to, to make things sound credible. The next thing, uh, oh, and the same goes for case studies. You know, if you don't have testimonials, at least get case studies, you know, Joe Smith was a down and out bartender and he took my money secret. And the next thing I knew is making $6,000 a month and, uh, you know, and then, and talk a little bit about how he did it. Something, just again, something to prove the case. And then we are, we know, we talked about guarantees and why there's so important and how they, you know, they add to your credibility. Um, the next thing would be to, to trust your right or more than you do a lot. I mean, I don't know how many clients have done foolish things without ever consulting me or asking me. And I know every writer's had this experience. You know, you turn in a headline that says how to, you know, make a million dollars in real estate and the client looks at that headline and says, well, you know, the word how to really isn't necessary. So I'll just cut that off. So now it says make a million dollars in real estate and a little change like that doesn't seem like much, but it's catastrophic to your response because the words how to are simply proven to increase response. You know, it doesn't matter why it works or how it works. All it matters is the copywriter knows that. That's why you put it in there. So if you're going to make any, even the smallest change to the copy, at least consult the writer before you do it. I've had clients butcher stuff, put it in the mail and then blame me for the result when I never saw the package again until I got it in the mailbox and went, oh my God, they ruined it. This is ironic that they'll pay a fortune to have you write something. Right. Uh, stensibly because you know what you're doing and, uh, then once it's written, they treat it so cavalierly. Yeah. Well, and they don't, and it's not even, and I'm not talking about, you know, the, the corner business doing to this me. This is happening with two of the biggest mailers in the country, two of the most successful companies around companies, every person listening to this call is heard of and is probably bought products from where they had some, some inexperienced person in charge of the thing. And they thought, Hey, this sounds better. And they just changed it. And, you know, and disaster ensued. And of course, if, and if you're a copywriter, it's one thing, this is disastrous for your business, but if you're a copywriter, it can be disastrous for your reputation. Cause now at those two companies, this, that these two examples happened fortunately years ago when I was, when I wasn't aware that I really needed to assert myself and say, no, you're not changing my copy. The next thing you know, your, your, your name is mud because the, the response won't, you know, it was terrible. And they said, well, you know, this guy doesn't work. Uh, when it wasn't even your work that failed, it was, you know, one of the companies I worked with, they had a guy that his only experience was in the lit, was in lists. And one of the things he told me was, you know, I really, I really don't think testimonials work. So we're just going to delete this testimonial you have here. And I foolishly went along with it. You know, he was the client. He's paying the bill. Okay, we'll take it out. And in retrospect, I look back and think, ah, you know, I don't know that that killed the promotion, but it was an incredibly stupid thing to do. I was there. And actually had a situation where, um, they had finalized the copy. They had finalized the art. And it went to press and flowers on the press. Uh, the client had second thoughts and, and changed the headline. Oh, so she didn't know this had happened. Right. And so after a few weeks, uh, the client calls up after a few weeks, Carline receives the package in the mail and, uh, just went ballistic. Right. And then a few weeks later, the client says, well, your package didn't work. And Carline says, Oh, did you mail my package? All I saw was this piece of crap that you guys put out. They haven't mailed my package yet. This day she refuses to work with that client. Yeah. And that's probably a good strategy. I'll tell you, you know, I was, I throughout my career, I mean, if there's, if there's been any, any, uh, what's the word I'm looking for? Character flaw. I have suffered from it. I'm too damn nice. My client screws things up and I'm like, Oh, well, you know, and if you're going to be a successful copywriter, you can't be a nice guy when it comes to things like that, you have got to be an asshole. And, and, and, and tell you, you know, and if your client won't go along with it, then yeah, you got to dump the client because there's nothing, I mean, your reputation is so all important. I mean, the only reason, you know, people want Clayton make peace is cause they, it'd be, and it can't get him is because they know he's got this incredible reputation. Whereas if you had gone along and said, Oh, well, yeah, change my copy, do this, you know, your results would be a third of what they've been, and you would be, you know, just your run of the mill, you know, A or B copywriter that, uh, never achieved fame and fortune because you let your client dictate what he was going to do. And I think, uh, that's one lesson that copywriters need to learn when they're really young, even if you're not sure. A lot of times there's been times that were, ah, maybe the client's right, but you got to go with your gut. You got to go with what you know is right and you got to take your lumps when you're wrong, but don't let your client. Dictating. Now that, you know what, then everything has to be said with a caveat. There are times when your client knows best, when your client knows the market, but those kinds of things need to be hashed out before the copy's written. You know, where you talk about platforms and themes and, and, and things of that nature. So you and your client are on the same page, but once the copy's written, and you know, like you said, on the press, you know, at that point, the client cannot come in there and change things or else your, uh, you know, your reputation is going to wind up as mud. And, and you don't need that. I mean, I'll just slow your progress to the, you know, as you ascend the copywriting, uh, uh, whatever, the copywriting scale. And, and again, for clients too, you're, you're, you're doing yourself in when you do that. Um, so let's see the other thing would be, uh, a lot of when it comes, this doesn't really apply to direct marketing businesses that we usually deal with, but back to the local businesses or the, the companies that aren't really involved in traditional direct response is this problem of worrying about your image, uh, in your community or among your friends or with your family. So you really have to make a decision. Do I, you know, is my image more important than my profits? And if you want to be a pillar in the community, maybe you can't run an ad that says, you know, the dirty little secret that every chiropractor wants to keep under the rug, you know, that you're running in your local paper. Yeah, maybe that will damage your pillar of the community status, but then you have to decide, do I want, you know, do I want pillar of the community or do I want to be fat with profits? And, uh, you know, I know another example of, you know, we've hammered go daddy because of the sex thing, but it is pretty much, I think I won't say proven, but there's a lot of evidence that putting a picture of a pretty girl in a long copy ad will increase your response. So if you're selling tax services, uh, and you just took a gratuitous net, Gary Halbert apparently tested this, you stick a gratuitous picture of a beautiful young woman in your ad, uh, your response will increase. Even though she's got nothing to do with it, and the ad of course has to be solid and good and everything else, but it gets people to take a look at your ad and then they read it. And if they're interested, they continue reading it. Um, but a lot of people resist because it makes them look bad. You know, if you're the tax guy and you've got this buck, some young woman in your ad, you know, it can make your wife mad. It can make your mother in law embarrassed or whatever. And again, then you have to decide, do I want, you know, do I want the image or do I want the money? And that, and I always advise people go for the money, forget your blasted image. You probably don't have an image anyway. Have you ever done a promotion for Baptist church? No. I wish I had though. Yeah. There's times when a bus and young lady in an ad probably won't help all that much. Yeah, that's right. But the point is a lot of people too, they put all this value on their image that they don't even have. Nobody knows who you are. Nobody cares who you are. You know, all they care about is what you can do for them. And obviously there are exceptions. There's exceptions to everything. But for most people, you know, your image is non-existent and money is more important than, oh, my mother in law might think badly of me if I have a, you know, whatever in my ad or have a long copy, non-pretty. Because that's the thing. Our advertising is not pretty generally. It's a lot of words. It's stuff that everyone says doesn't work, which always amuses me when people say, oh, that stuff doesn't work. And I said, well, and why did a client pay me $100,000 last year based on response if this stuff doesn't work? You know, and, you know, so I think that, but, you know, those are the critical lessons I think I've got for, you know, in the time we have. That's great. It's good stuff. It's interesting to talk to somebody who's worked, you know, in my industry, but also with companies that don't have strong direct response backgrounds, because it brings a whole new dimension to what we try to do in the total package. Well, it's a shame too, because there's so many opportunities in those kinds of businesses. I mean, in the businesses we work in, you've got a dozen copywriters beating each other over the head, competing, trying to eke out, you know, an extra, you know, .3% response, whereas I could go down to, you know, the local mortgage broker and probably double his response in a month, you know, and, you know, without any competition with mediocre copy, with half-assed marketing, but they won't hear of it. They won't do it. They're just, they're too myopic, too closed-minded to, you know, suffer from tunnel vision. There's one way to do it. They've always done it that way, and they're never going to change. If there was a way I could become king and get a law passed, it would be that every company in America from the smallest little dry cleaner on up to the largest Fortune 500 company would learn and use the basic tenants of direct response marketing, because it seems that somebody as smart as a business person who, whether he's a small business person who found a way to become his own boss, or whether it's a guy running Microsoft, it would seem that he would be smart enough to understand that if these techniques work in promotions that are scientifically trackable, then there's a very good chance that the same principles would increase response to his advertising. Right. And, uh, but somehow that never quite crosses the blood brain barrier. Well, and the other big thing too is a lot of these business people, you know, everyone dreams about opening their own business. I don't know how many people who think that having your own business is the easy life, but most of the guys I know or know of in business, they basically have bought themselves a job and they're working 40, 60, 80, 90, 100 hours a week with no hope of it ever changing. And most of the post of that's because they just don't have enough business. You know, they're trying to drum up business. They're trying to make sales or trying to keep things together. But if you can use these techniques, you know, to die, you know, I suppose you can come up with a technique that doubles your sales, you know, in six months. You know, wouldn't that mean then you could back off your hours by at least a third and still keep up your standard of living? Of course it would. And, you know, I think there's just so many advantages to this beyond just the money. I mean, you'll definitely make more money, but you'll free up your time. You'll be able to see your family. I mean, all these things that the supposed dream of having your small business really only comes true if you use the principles properly. Not only there's other things that come into it like managing your time, but all that's, you know, beyond the scope of our discussion. Great. Brad, thank you very much for your time. Okay. Great. All right, buddy. Thanks a lot. All right. Thank you, Clayton.
